lessons.title: Thread Basics
lessons.summary: Learn the basics of Java multithreading. Understand thread creation by extending the Thread class and implementing the Runnable interface, starting threads with start(), and the thread lifecycle.

contents.title: What Is Multithreading?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- What a [[thread]] is (a unit of concurrent execution in a program)
- [[Extending]] the Thread class and implementing the [[Runnable]] [[interface]]
- The difference between the start() and run() [[methods]]

You will learn the concept of threads, which form the foundation of **concurrent processing**.

contents.title: Extending Thread and Implementing Runnable
contents.body:
There are two main ways to create a thread.
Method 1: Extend the Thread class and [[override]] the run() method.
Method 2: Implement the Runnable interface, define the run() method, and pass it to the Thread [[constructor]].
Since Runnable is a SAM (Single Abstract Method) interface, it can also be written using a [[lambda expression]].
Implementing Runnable is generally recommended (because Java supports single inheritance only, extending Thread prevents extending other classes).
Calling start() executes run() in a separate thread. Calling run() directly only executes it synchronously in the current thread.
contents.note: The difference between start() and run() is a frequently tested topic. start() launches a new thread, while run() executes in the current thread.

contents.title: Starting Threads and Important Notes
contents.body:
The start() method can only be called once. Calling start() twice on the same thread throws an IllegalThreadStateException.
The execution order of threads is not guaranteed. Which thread runs first depends on the JVM and OS scheduling.
Thread.currentThread() returns a reference to the currently executing thread.
You can set a thread's name with setName() and retrieve it with getName().
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Thread Lifecycle
contents.body:
A thread has the following states (Thread.State enum).
| State | Description |
|---|---|
| NEW | The thread has been created but start() has not been called. |
| RUNNABLE | The thread is eligible to run (either running or waiting to run). |
| BLOCKED | The thread is waiting to acquire a monitor lock. |
| WAITING | The thread is in a waiting state (transitioned via Object.wait(), Thread.join(), LockSupport.park()). |
| TIMED_WAITING | The thread is waiting for a specified time (transitioned via Thread.sleep(), wait(timeout)). |
| TERMINATED | The run() method has completed execution. |
contents.note: There are 6 states obtainable via getState(). The RUNNABLE state includes both running and ready-to-run threads.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Thread Basics
contents.body:
The Gold exam tests the difference between start() and run(). Calling run() directly does not start a new thread.
Calling start() twice on the same thread throws an IllegalThreadStateException.
Thread execution order is nondeterministic, so it is important to understand the behavior of code whose output order is uncertain.
Memorize the 6 states of the Thread.State enum and their transition conditions.
Knowing that Runnable can be written as a lambda expression is also essential.

quizzes.question: Which statement about the Thread class is correct?
quizzes.explanation: The start() method can only be called once. Calling start() twice on the same thread throws an IllegalThreadStateException. Calling run() directly does not start a new thread; it executes synchronously in the current thread.

choices.text: Calling start() twice on the same thread throws an IllegalThreadStateException
Calling run() directly executes it in a new thread
start() can be called any number of times
Threads can only be created by extending the Thread class

quizzes.question: Which of the following is NOT included in Thread.State?
quizzes.explanation: The values of Thread.State are NEW, RUNNABLE, BLOCKED, WAITING, TIMED_WAITING, and TERMINATED — a total of 6. There is no state called RUNNING. The running state is included within RUNNABLE.

quizzes.question: To create a thread from an object that implements the Runnable interface, use the form new Thread(___).
quizzes.explanation: By passing an object that implements the Runnable interface to the Thread constructor, its run() method can be executed in a separate thread. Since Runnable is a functional interface, lambda expressions can also be passed.

lessons.title: Thread Control
lessons.summary: Learn thread control methods (sleep, join, yield), thread priority, and daemon threads. Understand how threads cooperate with each other.

contents.title: Overview of Thread Control
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[Thread]] control with sleep(), join(), and yield()
- Thread state transitions (NEW → RUNNABLE → TERMINATED, etc.)
- The interrupt() mechanism

Let's understand the thread lifecycle.

contents.title: sleep() and join()
contents.body:
Thread.sleep(long millis) pauses the current thread for the specified number of milliseconds. It is a [[static method]].
If interrupt() is called during sleep(), an InterruptedException is thrown.
join() waits for the target thread to finish. It is an [[instance]] [[method]].
join(long millis) allows waiting with a timeout.
join() can also throw an InterruptedException.
sleep() operates on the current thread and cannot pause other threads.
contents.note: sleep() is a static method that pauses the current thread. join() is an instance method that waits for the target thread to finish.

contents.title: Thread Priority and yield()
contents.body:
Thread.yield() temporarily pauses the current thread and gives other threads an opportunity to execute.
However, yield() is just a hint to the scheduler, and there is no guarantee that the thread will actually switch.
Thread priority is set using setPriority() with an integer between 1 and 10.
Thread.MIN_PRIORITY = 1, Thread.NORM_PRIORITY = 5, Thread.MAX_PRIORITY = 10.
Priority is a hint to the scheduler and does not guarantee execution order.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Daemon Threads
contents.body:
A daemon thread runs in the background, with the garbage collector being one such example.
Use setDaemon(true) to set a thread as a daemon. This must be done before calling start().
When all user (non-daemon) threads finish, daemon threads are forcibly stopped and the JVM exits.
Calling setDaemon() after start() throws an IllegalThreadStateException.
Threads created by a daemon thread are also daemon threads by default.
contents.note: Execution of the [[finally]] block is also not guaranteed in daemon threads. Do not use them for critical cleanup operations.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Thread Control
contents.body:
Remember that sleep() is a static method that always pauses the current thread.
join() is an instance method that waits for the target thread to finish.
setDaemon() must be called before start(); calling it after start() throws an [[exception]].
yield() and priority are hints to the scheduler and do not guarantee behavior.
InterruptedException is a checked exception, and it must be handled with sleep() and join().

quizzes.question: Which statement about Thread.sleep() is correct?
quizzes.explanation: Thread.sleep() is a static method that pauses the currently executing thread for a specified duration. It cannot pause other threads. InterruptedException is a checked exception and must be handled.

choices.text: It is a static method that pauses the current thread
It is an instance method that pauses the target thread
A thread in sleep() enters the BLOCKED state

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: The join() method causes the main thread to wait for thread t to complete. Therefore, "Hello" from thread t is always printed first, followed by "World". Without join(), the output order would be nondeterministic.

quizzes.question: Select the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: setDaemon() must be called before start(). Calling setDaemon() after start() throws an IllegalThreadStateException. The daemon setting must be configured before the thread is started.

choices.text: setDaemon() is called after start() (it must be set before start)
The lambda expression syntax is incorrect
The main method requires a throws declaration

quizzes.question: Complete the code to execute a task using ExecutorService.
quizzes.explanation: Executors.newFixedThreadPool() creates a fixed-size thread pool, and submit() submits a task. execute() is also valid, but submit() returns a Future.

lessons.title: Synchronization
lessons.summary: Learn about synchronization to maintain data integrity in a multithreaded environment. Understand the synchronized keyword, monitor locks, and the concept of race conditions.

contents.title: The Need for Synchronization
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Mutual exclusion with [[synchronized]]
- **Race conditions** and how to prevent them
- Inter-[[thread]] communication with wait(), notify(), and notifyAll()

This is how multiple threads safely share data.

contents.title: Synchronized Methods and Blocks
contents.body:
A synchronized method makes the entire method a target for locking.
For [[instance]] methods, the instance itself (this) is the lock target.
For [[static methods]], the Class object of the [[class]] is the lock target.
A synchronized block allows you to specify any object as the lock target.
The lock is acquired at the beginning of the block and automatically released when the block ends (including [[exceptions]]).
Only one thread at a time can access a synchronized region with the same lock.
contents.note: A synchronized method locks the entire method. A synchronized block can lock a more fine-grained scope.

contents.title: Race Condition Example
contents.body:
A race condition occurs when multiple threads read and write data simultaneously.
An operation like count++ is actually three steps: "read → add → write" and is not atomic (indivisible).
Without synchronization, two threads may read the same value, add to it, and produce an unexpected result.
Using synchronized prevents this problem.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: Monitor Locks and Reentrancy
contents.body:
The lock used by Java's synchronized is called a monitor lock (also known as an intrinsic lock).
Every Java object has a monitor.
Monitor locks are reentrant. If the same thread already holds a lock, it can reacquire the same lock.
This allows a synchronized method to call another synchronized method on the same object.
Without reentrancy, a thread would **deadlock** on its own lock.
contents.note: Reentrancy is an important property of Java locks.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Synchronization
contents.body:
Distinguish that synchronized on an instance method locks on this, while synchronized on a static method locks on the Class object.
Synchronized methods on different instances have different lock targets and can run concurrently.
Specifying null as the lock target in a synchronized block throws a [[NullPointerException]].
The [[volatile]] keyword guarantees visibility but not atomicity.
The Gold exam includes questions about determining whether a race condition occurs.

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes the output of the following code?
quizzes.explanation: Because the inc() method is not synchronized, a race condition can occur. c++ consists of three operations: "read → add → write" and is not atomic. Therefore, the result may be a nondeterministic value of 2000 or less.

choices.text: Since it is not synchronized, the result may be a nondeterministic value of 2000 or less

quizzes.question: Which is the correct combination regarding the lock target of synchronized methods?
quizzes.explanation: An instance synchronized method uses the instance (this) as the lock target, while a static synchronized method uses the Class object of that class as the lock target. This distinction is frequently tested.

choices.text: Instance method = locks on this, static method = locks on the Class object
Instance method = locks on the Class object, static method = locks on this
Both lock on the this object
Both lock on the Class object

quizzes.question: Which statement about Java monitor locks is correct?
quizzes.explanation: Java's monitor locks are reentrant. If the same thread already holds a lock, it can reacquire the lock on the same object. This allows synchronized methods to safely call other synchronized methods on the same object.

choices.text: The same thread can reacquire the same lock (reentrant)
Even the same thread will deadlock when reacquiring the same lock
Only objects inside a synchronized block have a monitor lock
Monitor locks must be explicitly acquired with the lock() method

quizzes.question: Arrange the thread lifecycle in the correct order.
quizzes.explanation: A thread follows this sequence: creation → start with start() → run() executes → wait for completion with join(). If run() is called directly without start(), it executes in the same thread.

lessons.title: Concurrent Collections
lessons.summary: Learn about collections that are safe to use in multithreaded environments (ConcurrentHashMap, CopyOnWriteArrayList, BlockingQueue). Understand the differences from traditional collections.

contents.title: The Need for Concurrent Collections
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Concurrent collections such as [[ConcurrentHashMap]] and CopyOnWriteArrayList
- Differences from regular collections
- Avoiding ConcurrentModificationException

Achieve safe **concurrent processing** with **thread-safe** collections.

contents.title: ConcurrentHashMap
contents.body:
ConcurrentHashMap is a **thread-safe** implementation of HashMap.
Internally optimized locking mechanisms (fine-grained locking at the bucket level and CAS operations) achieve high concurrency.
Read operations are performed without locks, and write operations are locked at the bucket (bin) level.
Null keys and null values are not allowed (a difference from HashMap).
Atomic operation [[methods]] such as putIfAbsent(), replace(), and compute() are provided.
Iterators are weakly consistent and do not throw ConcurrentModificationException.
contents.note: ConcurrentHashMap prohibits null keys and null values. Note the difference from HashMap.

contents.title: CopyOnWriteArrayList
contents.body:
CopyOnWriteArrayList is a **thread-safe** List that creates a copy of the internal [[array]] on every write.
Read operations are fast and performed without locks.
Every write operation (add, set, remove) creates a copy of the entire internal array, so it is suitable for scenarios with few writes.
Iterators reference a snapshot from the time of creation, so changes during iteration are not reflected.
The iterator's remove() throws UnsupportedOperationException.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: BlockingQueue
contents.body:
BlockingQueue is a queue used for passing data between [[threads]].
When the queue is empty, take() blocks (waits) until data is added.
When the queue is full, put() blocks until space becomes available.
Major implementations include ArrayBlockingQueue (fixed size) and LinkedBlockingQueue (variable size).
It is ideal for implementing the Producer-Consumer pattern.
offer() and poll() are non-blocking alternatives (timeout specification is also possible).
contents.note: BlockingQueue is the foundation of the Producer-Consumer pattern. The blocking behavior of put/take is tested on exams.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Concurrent Collections
contents.body:
Make sure to remember these key characteristics: ConcurrentHashMap prohibits null, CopyOnWriteArrayList iterators use snapshots, and BlockingQueue's put/take block.
Collections.synchronizedList() wraps a list manually, but external synchronization is required during iteration.
CopyOnWriteArrayList is suitable for scenarios with many reads and few writes.
The behavior of ConcurrentHashMap's compute()-family methods may also be tested.
It is also important that concurrent collection iterators do not throw ConcurrentModificationException.

quizzes.question: Which statement about ConcurrentHashMap is correct?
quizzes.explanation: ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null keys or null values. This is an important difference from HashMap (which allows both null keys and null values). A NullPointerException is thrown.

choices.text: Neither null keys nor null values are allowed
Null keys are allowed but null values are not
Like HashMap, null keys and null values are allowed

quizzes.question: Which statement about the CopyOnWriteArrayList iterator is correct?
quizzes.explanation: The CopyOnWriteArrayList iterator references a snapshot of the array at the time of creation. Changes made to the list after the iterator is created are not reflected. Additionally, the iterator's remove() throws UnsupportedOperationException.

choices.text: It references a snapshot at creation time, and subsequent changes are not reflected
Changes to the list are reflected in real time
Elements can be removed from the list using the iterator's remove()
It throws ConcurrentModificationException when changes are made

quizzes.question: Select the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: ConcurrentHashMap does not allow null values. Executing put("key2", null) throws a NullPointerException. HashMap allows null keys and null values, but ConcurrentHashMap prohibits them.

choices.text: Attempting to store a null value in ConcurrentHashMap
ConcurrentHashMap constructor requires a capacity parameter
The add() method should be used instead of put()
The import statement for ConcurrentHashMap is incorrect

lessons.title: ExecutorService
lessons.summary: Learn the ExecutorService framework for managing thread pools. Understand Executors factory methods, the difference between submit() and execute(), Future and Callable, and shutdown methods.

contents.title: Overview of ExecutorService
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- [[Thread]] pool management with [[ExecutorService]]
- Retrieving asynchronous results with [[Future]]<T>
- Types of **thread pools** (fixed, cached, single, etc.)

A safer and more efficient approach than managing threads directly.

contents.title: Executors Factory Methods
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| newSingleThreadExecutor() | Executes tasks sequentially with 1 thread |
| newFixedThreadPool(n) | Fixed-size pool of n threads |
| newCachedThreadPool() | Creates threads as needed, reuses idle ones |
| newScheduledThreadPool(n) | Supports delayed and periodic execution |
All return an ExecutorService interface.
You must call shutdown() to release resources when done.
contents.note: If more tasks are submitted than the pool size, they wait in a queue until a thread becomes available.

contents.title: submit() and Callable/Future
contents.body:
execute() takes a [[Runnable]] (no return value) and returns nothing.
submit() takes a Runnable or [[Callable]] and returns a Future<T>.
Callable<T> has a call() [[method]] that can return a result and is a [[functional interface]].
Future.get() retrieves the result, blocking until it is available.
Future.get(long timeout, TimeUnit unit) allows retrieval with a timeout.
Callable's call() can throw [[checked exceptions]], which are wrapped as ExecutionException.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: shutdown() and shutdownNow()
contents.body:
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| shutdown() | Stops accepting new tasks. Already submitted tasks are executed to completion |
| shutdownNow() | Attempts to interrupt running tasks and returns a list of waiting tasks |
| awaitTermination() | Waits for pool termination for a specified time |
| isShutdown() | Returns whether shutdown() has been called |
| isTerminated() | Returns whether all tasks have completed |
Submitting a task after shutdown() throws a RejectedExecutionException.
contents.note: If you forget to call shutdown(), the program will not terminate. Always call shutdown() in a try-[[finally]] block.

contents.title: Exam Prep: ExecutorService
contents.body:
Make sure to memorize the difference between execute() and submit() (return value, accepted types).
Callable<T>'s call() can throw checked [[exceptions]], but Runnable's run() cannot.
Future.get() is a blocking method that requires handling InterruptedException and ExecutionException.
Understand the difference between shutdown() and shutdownNow() (graceful vs. forceful).
It is essential to always call shutdown() when you are done with an ExecutorService.

quizzes.question: Which statement about the difference between execute() and submit() is correct?
quizzes.explanation: submit() returns a Future, allowing you to retrieve the task's result and completion status. execute() has no return value and only accepts Runnable. submit() accepts both Runnable and Callable.

choices.text: submit() returns a Future, but execute() has no return value
execute() returns a Future, but submit() has no return value
submit() only accepts Callable, and execute() only accepts Runnable

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: newSingleThreadExecutor() executes tasks sequentially with a single thread. Tasks are submitted with submit(), and shutdown() stops accepting new tasks but allows already submitted tasks to execute. f1.get() returns "A" and f2.get() returns "B", so the output is "AB".

quizzes.question: To execute a task that returns a result using ExecutorService, use the ___ interface.
quizzes.explanation: Callable<T> is a functional interface with a call() method that can return a value. You submit a task with ExecutorService's submit(Callable) and receive the result via Future<T>. Unlike Runnable, it can also throw checked exceptions.

quizzes.question: Select all correct thread-safe implementation approaches.
quizzes.explanation: synchronized blocks, Atomic types such as AtomicInteger, ConcurrentHashMap, and immutable objects are thread-safe implementation approaches. ArrayList is not thread-safe; when synchronization is needed, use Collections.synchronizedList() or CopyOnWriteArrayList.

lessons.title: Atomic Operations and Deadlock
lessons.summary: Learn about lock-free synchronization with atomic variables (AtomicInteger, AtomicReference), deadlock conditions and prevention, and inter-thread communication with wait()/notify().

contents.title: Atomic Operations and Deadlock
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Atomic operation [[classes]] such as AtomicInteger
- The CAS (Compare And Swap) mechanism
- **Deadlock** conditions and avoidance strategies

Learn about advanced **concurrent processing** problems and their solutions.

contents.title: AtomicInteger and AtomicReference
contents.body:
AtomicInteger is a class that performs atomic (indivisible) operations on int values.
It provides [[methods]] such as get(), set(), incrementAndGet(), decrementAndGet(), and compareAndSet().
compareAndSet(expected, newValue) updates the value to newValue only if the current value matches expected (CAS operation).
AtomicReference<T> is a class that performs atomic operations on [[reference types]].
These achieve **thread-safe** operations without using [[synchronized]].
Atomic [[variables]] make operations on a single variable [[thread]]-safe, but they do not guarantee atomicity for compound operations.
contents.note: AtomicInteger is more lightweight than synchronized and is ideal for simple counters.

contents.title: Deadlock
contents.body:
A **deadlock** is a situation where two or more threads are forever waiting for each other to release their locks.
There are four conditions for [[deadlock]]: mutual exclusion, hold and wait, no preemption, and circular wait.
A deadlock can occur when all four conditions are met.
The most common prevention strategy is to unify the lock acquisition order.
When a deadlock occurs, the program hangs and does not recover automatically.
contents.note: The "//" in the code are comments (explanatory text). They do not affect program execution.

contents.title: wait(), notify(), and notifyAll()
contents.body:
wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() are methods of the Object class used for inter-thread communication.
They can only be called inside a synchronized block. Calling them outside a synchronized block throws an IllegalMonitorStateException.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| wait() | Puts the current thread in a waiting state and releases the lock. |
| notify() | Wakes up one of the waiting threads (which one is nondeterministic). |
| notifyAll() | Wakes up all waiting threads. |
After returning from wait(), the thread must reacquire the lock.
contents.note: Calling wait() inside a while loop to check the condition is a best practice. This handles spurious wakeups.

contents.title: Exam Prep: Atomic Operations and Deadlock
contents.body:
Remember the difference between incrementAndGet() and getAndIncrement() in AtomicInteger (equivalent to prefix ++ and postfix ++).
Understand that compareAndSet() is a CAS operation that updates only when the expected value matches.
The most fundamental strategy for **deadlock** prevention is unifying the lock acquisition order.
The fact that wait()/notify() can only be used inside a synchronized block is frequently tested.
Also understand the importance of checking conditions with a while loop when using wait().

quizzes.question: Which statement correctly describes the behavior of AtomicInteger's compareAndSet(10, 20)?
quizzes.explanation: compareAndSet(expected, newValue) updates the value to newValue (20) only if the current value matches expected (10), and returns true. If they don't match, nothing is changed and false is returned. This is the CAS (Compare-And-Swap) operation.

choices.text: Updates to 20 only if the current value is 10, and returns true
Unconditionally changes the value from 10 to 20
Adds 10 to the current value and sets 20
Sets whichever is larger between 10 and 20

quizzes.question: Which statement about the use of wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() is correct?
quizzes.explanation: wait(), notify(), and notifyAll() can only be called inside a synchronized block (or method). Calling them outside a synchronized block throws an IllegalMonitorStateException. This is because these methods are related to monitor lock operations.

choices.text: They can only be called inside a synchronized block
They can be called freely from anywhere
They are methods of the Thread class
They are called as static methods

quizzes.question: Select the error in the following code.
quizzes.explanation: wait() can only be called inside a synchronized block. In this code, wait() is called outside a synchronized block, so an IllegalMonitorStateException is thrown at runtime. It should be written as synchronized (lock) { lock.wait(); }.

choices.text: wait() is called outside a synchronized block
The catch for InterruptedException is unnecessary
The lock object's type cannot be Object

lessons.title: CompletableFuture
lessons.summary: Learn asynchronous programming with CompletableFuture, introduced in Java 8. Cover supplyAsync, thenApply, thenCompose, allOf/anyOf, and other declarative approaches to asynchronous processing, along with key Gold exam points.

contents.title: What Is CompletableFuture?
contents.body:
In this lesson, you will learn:

- Asynchronous processing chains with [[CompletableFuture]]
- How to use thenApply(), thenAccept(), and thenCompose()
- Combining with [[exception]] handling (exceptionally, handle)

This is the standard approach for asynchronous programming in modern Java.

contents.title: Creating Asynchronous Tasks
contents.body:
There are three main ways to create a CompletableFuture.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| supplyAsync | Starts an asynchronous task with a return value. Runs on ForkJoinPool.commonPool() by default. |
| runAsync | Starts an asynchronous task without a return value (Runnable). |
| completedFuture | Wraps an already completed value into a CompletableFuture (useful for testing). |
Both supplyAsync and runAsync accept an Executor as a second argument to change the execution [[thread]] pool.
contents.note: Remember: supplyAsync = [[Supplier]] (with return value), runAsync = [[Runnable]] (without return value).

contents.title: Chaining Operations (thenApply / thenAccept / thenRun)
contents.body:
The greatest feature of CompletableFuture is the ability to chain operations.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| thenApply | Receives the previous result, transforms it, and returns a new value (equivalent to map). |
| thenAccept | Receives the previous result and consumes it (no return value). |
| thenRun | Executes another operation without using the previous result (no return value). |
| thenCompose | Receives the previous result and returns a new CompletableFuture (equivalent to flatMap). |
Each [[method]] has an Async variant (such as thenApplyAsync) that can be executed on a different thread.
contents.code:
CompletableFuture<String> result = CompletableFuture
    .supplyAsync(() -> "java")
    .thenApply(s -> s.toUpperCase())       // "JAVA"
    .thenApply(s -> s + " Programming");  // "JAVA Programming"
System.out.println(result.get()); // JAVA Programming

// thenAccept: consume the result (no return value)
CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> 100)
    .thenAccept(n -> System.out.println("Result: " + n)); // Result: 100

// thenCompose: chain operations that return CompletableFuture
CompletableFuture<String> composed = CompletableFuture
    .supplyAsync(() -> 1)
    .thenCompose(id -> CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(
        () -> "User-" + id  // Another async operation using the id
    ));
System.out.println(composed.get()); // User-1
contents.note: Think of thenApply as Stream.map() and thenCompose as Stream.flatMap() for easier understanding.

contents.title: CompletableFuture Composition Methods Overview
contents.body:
| Method | Input | Output | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| thenApply() | Previous result | Transformed value | Transform the result |
| thenAccept() | Previous result | void | Consume the result |
| thenRun() | None | void | Execute a follow-up action |
| thenCompose() | Previous result | CompletableFuture | Chain asynchronous operations |
| exceptionally() | Exception | Alternative value | Error handling |

Think of thenApply = map and thenCompose = flatMap to see the similarity with the [[Stream API]].
contents.note: Async-suffixed methods (such as thenApplyAsync) are executed on a different thread.

contents.title: Composing Multiple Asynchronous Operations
contents.body:
There are also ways to combine multiple CompletableFutures.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| allOf | Waits for all CompletableFutures to complete. Returns CompletableFuture<Void>. |
| anyOf | Returns the result when any one completes. Returns CompletableFuture<Object>. |
| thenCombine | Combines the results of two CompletableFutures and returns a new value. |
contents.note: Since allOf returns Void, individual results must be obtained by calling get() on the original CompletableFutures.

contents.title: Exception Handling and Exam Points
contents.body:
Exception handling in CompletableFuture uses exceptionally and handle.
| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| exceptionally | Returns an alternative value only when an exception occurs. Not called on normal completion. |
| handle | Handles both normal and error cases. The first argument is the result, the second is the exception (may be null). |
| whenComplete | Receives both result and exception for side effects, but does not transform the value. |

[Gold Exam Points]
- The difference in return types between supplyAsync and runAsync
- The difference between thenApply and thenCompose ([[Function]] vs Function returning a CF)
- allOf returns **CompletableFuture**<Void>
- get() throws ExecutionException (and InterruptedException)
- getNow(defaultValue) returns a default value without blocking
contents.note: The argument to exceptionally may be wrapped in a CompletionException rather than the original exception. getMessage() may include the nested exception name.

quizzes.question: What is the return type of CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> "Hello")?
quizzes.explanation: supplyAsync takes a Supplier<T> and returns a CompletableFuture<T>. Since the lambda returns "Hello" (a String), the return type is CompletableFuture<String>. runAsync returns CompletableFuture<Void>.

quizzes.question: Predict the output of the following code.
quizzes.explanation: supplyAsync generates "hello" asynchronously → thenApply transforms it to "HELLO" → thenApply transforms it to "HELLO!". get() retrieves the final result, so the output is "HELLO!".

quizzes.question: What is the correct return type of CompletableFuture.allOf(cf1, cf2, cf3)?
quizzes.explanation: The return type of allOf() is CompletableFuture<Void>. allOf waits for all passed CompletableFutures to complete but does not aggregate individual results. To obtain each result, call get() individually on the original CompletableFutures. anyOf() returns CompletableFuture<Object>.
